summary: Philosophical school of epistemology holding that all knowledge derives from sensory experience, often contrasted with rationalism. Key figures include John Locke, David Hume, and George Berkeley. In quizbowl, the topic is dominated by Quine's 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism' and related discussions of logical positivism, the Vienna Circle, and Wilfrid Sellars's 'Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind.'

works.name: Two Dogmas of Empiricism (Quine)
works.description: W. V. O. Quine's 1951 paper 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism' is the single most clued text under this answerline. It attacks two central tenets of logical positivism: the analytic-synthetic distinction and reductionism. Quine uses the examples of 'no unmarried man is married' vs. 'no bachelor is married' to discuss cognitive synonymy and analyticity. He argues that no individual statement is empirically verifiable on its own (confirmation holism / Duhem-Quine thesis), compares physical objects to Greek gods, and imagines science as a 'force field' with experience at the boundary. Paul Grice and P. F. Strawson wrote a 'Defense' of analyticity in response; Hilary Putnam 'Revisited' it.

clues.clue: attacks the analytic-synthetic distinction and reductionism as two 'dogmas'

title references the concepts of reductionism and the analytic-synthetic distinction

examples: discusses cognitive synonymy with the example of an 'unmarried man' and a 'bachelor'
distinguishes between two kinds of statements with the examples 'no unmarried man is married' and 'no bachelor is married'

clues.clue: confirmation holism — no statement verifiable on its own; all of science tested together (Duhem-Quine thesis)
examples: no statement is empirically verifiable on its own due to the interconnectedness of science, known as confirmation holism

clues.clue: criticizes Rudolf Carnap's state-descriptions and The Logical Structure of the World (Aufbau)
examples: criticizes thinkers like Rudolf Carnap for claiming that each 'meaningful statement is equivalent to some logical construct'

examples: imagines science as a force field with experience at the boundary and 'recalcitrant truths' closer to the edges

examples: A 'defense' of a concept criticized in this paper was written by Paul Grice and P.F. Strawson
notes that 'the word definition has come to have a dangerously reassuring sound'

works.name: Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind (Sellars)
works.description: Wilfrid Sellars's 1956 paper 'Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind' introduced the 'Myth of the Given' — an attack on the foundationalist idea that basic sensory concepts can both provide knowledge and justify beliefs. It also introduced the 'logical space of reasons,' arguing that characterizing a state as knowing is not giving an empirical description but placing it in a space where justification is expected. John McDowell's Mind and World builds on Sellars's critique.

clues.clue: 'Myth of the Given' — attacks the existence of basic concepts that both provide and justify knowledge
examples: attacks the existence of basic concepts that also provide justification as the 'myth of the given'
Sellars introduced 'The Myth of the Given'

clues.clue: 'logical space of reasons' — characterizing a state as knowing is not empirical description but placing it in a justificatory space
examples: 'characterizing a state as that of knowing' is not 'giving an empirical description,' but instead involves 'placing it' in this hypothetical location

works.name: Logical Positivism / Vienna Circle
works.description: Logical positivism (logical empiricism) was the philosophy of the Vienna Circle, led by Moritz Schlick and Rudolf Carnap, which embraced the verification principle and rejected metaphysics. A. J. Ayer's Language, Truth, and Logic brought it to Anglophone readers. It declined after Thomas Kuhn's Structure of Scientific Revolutions. Wittgenstein's Tractatus heavily influenced it. Otto Neurath analogized scientific knowledge to a ship rebuilt while sailing. Bas van Fraassen's 'constructive empiricism' is an anti-realist successor.

clues.clue: Vienna Circle — Moritz Schlick, Rudolf Carnap; verification principle; rejected metaphysics
examples: Moritz Schlick and Rudolf Carnap were central members
embraced the verification principle and was championed by the Vienna Circle

clues.clue: A. J. Ayer's Language, Truth, and Logic brought logical positivism to Anglophone readers
examples: Ayer's Language, Truth, and Logic brought this group's call for the 'elimination of metaphysics' to Anglophone readers

clues.clue: Bas van Fraassen's 'constructive empiricism' — theories only strive for 'empirical adequacy'
examples: An anti-realist form of this position holds that theories only strive to reach a namesake form of 'adequacy'

clues.clue: Helen Longino's 'contextual empiricism' — selecting between underdetermined theories requires diverse community
examples: selecting between undetermined scientific theories requires critical revision from a diverse community

works.name: General / Definition and Key Figures
works.description: Empiricism is defined as the epistemological stance that all knowledge derives from experience, contrasted with rationalism which holds that some knowledge is innate. Key figures include John Locke (tabula rasa), David Hume (impressions and ideas), and George Berkeley. The concept traces back to Aristotle's notion of the mind as tabula rasa at birth. Leibniz critiqued a central work of empiricism (Locke's Essay) to defend innate ideas. Hume's 'missing shade of blue' is a famous counterexample from within the tradition. William James characterized empiricists as 'tough-minded, pessimistic, and skeptical.' Kant claimed to have ended the rationalism-empiricism debate.

clues.clue: all knowledge derives from sensory experience; contrasted with rationalism

sensory perception is the only way to obtain knowledge

clues.clue: key figures: Locke, Hume, Berkeley; traces to Aristotle's tabula rasa

clues.clue: William James: empiricists are 'tough-minded, pessimistic, and skeptical'

comprehensive_summary:
Empiricism as a quizbowl answerline is dominated by two major texts: Quine's 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism' and Sellars's 'Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind,' along with questions about logical positivism and the Vienna Circle.

Quine's paper attacks the analytic-synthetic distinction and reductionism. The bachelor/unmarried man example illustrates cognitive synonymy. Quine argues for confirmation holism (no individual statement is testable alone — the Duhem-Quine thesis) and compares physical objects to Greek gods. He criticizes Carnap's Aufbau and state-descriptions. Grice and Strawson wrote a 'Defense' of analyticity in response.

Sellars's paper introduced the 'Myth of the Given' (attacking foundationalism) and the 'logical space of reasons.' McDowell's Mind and World extends Sellars's critique.

Logical positivism (the Vienna Circle: Schlick, Carnap, Neurath, Feigl) embraced the verification principle and rejected metaphysics. Ayer's Language, Truth, and Logic popularized it in English. Wittgenstein's Tractatus was a key influence. The movement declined after Kuhn.

As a general concept, empiricism holds that all knowledge derives from experience, contrasted with rationalism's innate ideas. Key figures are Locke (tabula rasa), Hume (impressions/ideas, missing shade of blue), and Berkeley. The tradition traces to Aristotle. Kant claimed to have resolved the empiricism-rationalism debate. Van Fraassen's 'constructive empiricism' and Longino's 'contextual empiricism' are modern variants.

cards.front: Work: attacks the analytic-synthetic distinction and reductionism; uses 'no bachelor is married' vs. 'no unmarried man is married' to discuss cognitive synonymy
cards.back: Two Dogmas of Empiricism (W. V. O. Quine)

cards.front: Concept: the position that no individual statement can be tested in isolation — all of science is tested together; named for its two formulators, one of whom wrote 'Two Dogmas'
Work: introduced the 'Myth of the Given' — an attack on the foundationalist idea that basic sensory concepts can both provide and justify knowledge; also introduced the 'logical space of reasons'
cards.back: Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind (Wilfrid Sellars)
cards.work: Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind (Sellars)

cards.front: Work: Paul Grice and P. F. Strawson wrote a 'Defense' of this concept after it was criticized in a Quine paper that also attacks reductionism
cards.back: the analytic-synthetic distinction (defended in response to 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism')

cards.front: Concept: an anti-realist position holding that scientific theories only strive for 'empirical adequacy' rather than truth; developed by Bas van Fraassen
